coredata.dc:title: Eu-polyaspartic acid based ratiometric fluorescent probes for ultrasensitive and visual detection of tetracycline and copper ions in food samples
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Feifan
Huang, Yue
Mei, Danling
Wang, Lei
Zhao, Xueqin
Huang, Biao
coredata.dc:description: The visualization and accurate on-site detection of tetracycline and Cu2+ residues in food are essential for maintaining food safety and public health. A novel poly (aspartic acid) Ratiometric fluorescent probe was designed for the sensitive visual detection of tetracycline (Tc) and Cu2+ using the EDTA-Eu3+ complex as the sensing unit and fluorescein dye as the internal standard. The fluorescence of Eu3+ at 617 nm was turned on significantly by Tc via the “antenna effect” and can be quenched by Cu2+ owing to the inner filter effect. The probes selectively detected Tc and Cu2+ over a wide linear range, low LODs of 10 nM and 333 nM, respectively, were obtained. A smartphone APP and probe-loaded test paper were also assembled into a biomass-based portable sensor for visual point-of-use analysis. The fabricated sensors successfully monitored the Tc and Cu2+ levels in milk, honey, and tap water samples, suggesting their broad applications in food safety monitoring and environmental analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Poly(aspartic) acid
Copper ion
Tetracycline
Ratiometric fluorescence
Smartphone sensing

Detection of Tc and Cu2+ in actual samples
The visual detection of Tc and Cu2+
Characterization and mechanism of PASP-FL-Eu (PFE) nano-probe
Fluorescence response to Tc and Cu2+
Visualization detection of Tc and Cu2+ based on Smartphone

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the synthesis of the Eu3+-PASP fluorescent probe. (b) Schematic diagram of the smartphone-assisted sensing platform used for quantitatively detecting Tc and Cu2+.
(a) Fluorescence excitation spectra. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra. (c) Fluorescence lifetime decay curve. (d) Energy transfer mechanism of Eu3+ luminescence in the present of Tc.

(a) Fluorescence spectra at different wavelengths from 350 to 420 nm. (b) Effect of pH on the fluorescence response of the PFE sensor. (c) Effect of the concentration of PFE on the fluorescence response for TCs detection. (d) Fluorescence intensity changes at 616 nm as a function of reaction time with the addition of 20 μM TCs at room temperature. (Different alphabets indicate significant differences, ρ < 0.05).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of PFE with the addition of various concentrations of Tc, (b) linear relationship of I617/I519 with the concentration of Tc, and (c) Corresponding CIE chromaticity diagram. (d)The FL recovery of PFE + Tc with the addition of various amounts of Cu2+, (b) the linear relationship between the I617/I519 versus Cu2+ concentration, and (c). Inset: Corresponding photos under 370 nm UV lamp.
(a) Fluorescence spectra and (b) I617/I519 of the reaction system in the present of Tc, Cu2+ and interfering substances, respectively.

(a) and (b) Digital photos of PFE test paper and PFE-Tc test paper respectively under daylight (upper) and UV lamps (middle and below). Below groups represent photos after the presence of Tc or Cu2+. (c) and (d) The Corresponding linear correlations between logarithm R/G and Concentration of Tc and Cu2+, respectively.
Comparison of different methods for Tc and Cu2+ sensing.

head.ce:title: Eu-polyaspartic acid based ratiometric fluorescent probes for ultrasensitive and visual detection of tetracycline and copper ions in food samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Sciences and Medicine, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou 310018, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Life Sciences and Medicine, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Life Sciences and Medicine, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou 310018, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The visualization and accurate on-site detection of tetracycline and Cu2+ residues in food are essential for maintaining food safety and public health. A novel poly (aspartic acid) Ratiometric fluorescent probe was designed for the sensitive visual detection of tetracycline (Tc) and Cu2+ using the EDTA-Eu3+ complex as the sensing unit and fluorescein dye as the internal standard. The fluorescence of Eu3+ at 617 nm was turned on significantly by Tc via the “antenna effect” and can be quenched by Cu2+ owing to the inner filter effect. The probes selectively detected Tc and Cu2+ over a wide linear range, low LODs of 10 nM and 333 nM, respectively, were obtained. A smartphone APP and probe-loaded test paper were also assembled into a biomass-based portable sensor for visual point-of-use analysis. The fabricated sensors successfully monitored the Tc and Cu2+ levels in milk, honey, and tap water samples, suggesting their broad applications in food safety monitoring and environmental analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In this regard, we report an innovative PAsp-based fluorescent sensor for the ratiometric detection of Tc and Cu2+ using ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) as a bridging ligand and fluorescein dye as a green-emitting fluorescent internal standard ( Fig. 1). The “on–off” sensing platform exhibited ratiometric fluorescence detection of Tc and Cu2+ in the micromolar concentration range by monitoring the fluorescence changes of Eu3+ at 617 nm and fluorescein at 519 nm. The performance of the sensing platform was verified for reliable detection of Tc and Cu2+ levels in milk, honey, and tap water samples. In addition, a smartphone-based portable platform with RGB color recognition software was designed for the point-of-care visual quantitative detection of Tc and Cu2+.

Unless otherwise noted, reagents and solvents were reagent grade and used without further purification. Polyaspartic acid (PASP MW 10,000), DMTMM, fluorescein sodium (FL), EuCl3.6H2O, Sodium citrate, bicarbonate, tetracycline hydrochloride (Tc), Calcium chloride (CaCl2), Ferric chloride (FeCl3), Kanamycins(KAN), Erythromycin(EM), Penicillin potassium(PG), Arginine (Arg), L-cysteine (Cys), Lysine (Lys), Glycine (Gly), Glutamate (Glu) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai) Co. ltd. Diethylenetriamine（DET）, copper nitrate (Cu(NO3)2), Sodium chloride (NaCl), Iron dichloride (FeCl2), Levofloxacin hydrochloride(LFX), Zinc chloride（ZnCl2）, Magnesium sulfate （MgSO4）, Histidine (His), Tyrosine （Tyr） was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. ltd (Shanghai, China). Citrate was purchased from Hangzhou Gaojing Fine Chemical Co. Ltd. (Zhejiang, China). EDTA disodium salt was purchased from Wuxi Prospect Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Jiangsu, China).

UV-Vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Nanodrop 2000 ultra-micro Spectrophotometer (Thermo, USA). The lifetime measurements were determined on a FluoroMax-4 spectrometer (HORIBA, USA). The luminescence spectra were recorded on a F97 fluorescence spectrophotometer (lengguang, China). 1H-NMR was measured using a WNR-400 nuclear magnetic resonance spectrometer (Zhongke-niujin, China).

Then, 0.75 g of sodium fluorescein was dissolved in deionized water, 0.19 g of EDC and 0.12 g of NHS were added to activate the carboxyl group for 30 min. The activated sodium fluorescein was added to the above PASP- EDTA solution（30 mL）and stirred for 12 hours. After dialysis and freeze-drying, the prepared PASP-FL-EDTA was stored at 4 ° C.

Finally, 0.45 g of purified PASP-FL - EDTA was dissolved in 5 mL of deionized water. 5 mL of EuCl3·6 H2O (255 μM) was dropwise to this solution and stirred for 3 h. The mixture PASP-FL-Eu（PFE） was dialysate and collected by freeze-dry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedures for the detection of Tc and Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: To optimize detection conditions, PFE (1 mg/mL) and the TC solution (500 μL, 20 μM) were used to assess the fluorescence intensity ratio of I617/I519 under the variable pH values, probes assumed volume, excitation wavelength and the response time. For Tc detection, 10 μg of PFE probe was dispersed in 10 μL of Tris-Hcl solution (0.05 M, pH 9.0). Different concentrations of Tc (0–20 μM) were added and the final volume of the solution was kept at 0.5 mL. After incubation of 5 min at room temperature, the fluorescence spectra were recorded and the intensity ratio at 619 nm and 445 nm (I619 /I445) was used for the quantitative analysis of Tc. For Cu2+ detection, 10 μL of PFE probe (1 mg / mL) and 500 μL Tc (20 μM) were added into a series of gradient concentrations of Cu2+(500 μL). After incubation of 5 min at room temperature, the fluorescence spectra were recorded. In addition, the effects of other interfering ions and antibiotics (20 μM) on the fluorescence intensity of the nanoprobe were compared under the same conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Tc and Cu2+ in actual samples
The visual detection of Tc and Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: Ordinary filter paper was cut into 6 mm diameter pieces using a hole punch and immersed in a 0.05 mg/mL PFE or PFE-Eu dispersion. After 0.5 h, it was left to dry at 50 °C. Next, 20 μL of a Tc standard solution with varying concentrations (0.5–50 μM) was added to the above test paper. The naked eye detected fluorescence color changes with the aid of a 365 nm UV lamp, and RGB values were analyzed using a color recognition APP Color Scoop. Similarly, PFE-Eu loaded test paper was added to a series of concentrations of Cu2+ solution (20 μL, 2–200 μM) and dried at 50 °C. Fluorescence photos were taken using a cell phone under a 365 nm UV lamp. RGB extraction was analyzed under low exposure conditions using the smartphone APP Color Scoop. And honey solutions with different concentrations of Tc and Cu2+ were prepared according to 2.4, detected using a paper-based sensor and analysed by RGB.

Statistical analysis was carried out using Origin 2021 and SPSS version 24.0. Statistical analysis was performed using two-tailed unpaired t-tests for comparison. The data were obtained from at least three independent experiments and expressed as means ± standard deviations (SD). Differences were statistically significant when the p-values were less than 0.05.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and mechanism of PASP-FL-Eu (PFE) nano-probe
ce:section.ce:para: Eco-friendly Eu3+-based polymers as ratiometric probes are important for food safety monitoring and environment analysis. The structure of PASP- FL – EDTA was firstly characterized by 1 H NMR. The position of the characteristic peaks (δ7.8∼ 6.6 ppm, δ 3.7 ppm and δ 2.4 ppm) indicated that PASP-FL-EDTA was composed of the EDTA and fluorescein ( Fig. S1 ). Upon an excitation of 365 nm, the fluorescence peak of fluorescein units appeared at 517 nm in the PFE solution and kept stable in the presence of Tc, suggesting the suitability as an internal standard for Ratiometric measurements. Meanwhile, the PFE solution exhibited the characteristic emission peaks of Eu3+ ions are 595 nm (5D0 → 7F1) and 616 nm (5D0 → 7F2). The 616 nm emission peak is sensitive to coordination environment and can be sharply turned on by Tc through the "antenna effect" of Tc sensitizing Eu3+ (Figs. 1 a, 1 d). The addition of Cu2+ greatly quenched the red fluorescence of Eu3+ in the PFE-Tc system. The luminescent mechanism between Tc and PFE was shown in Fig. 1 b. It can be manifested by UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence lifetimes. In UV spectrometry, Tc has typical absorption bands at 272 nm and 369 nm, when Tc co-existed with PFE, one blue-shifted to 265 nm and another one red-shifted to 397 nm due to the coordination of Tc and the PFE probe (blue line). A new peak at 360 nm is also observed. The UV–vis absorbance spectra of Cu2+ are observed without overlapping with the excitation spectrum of PFE-Tc, indicating no fluorescence resonance energy transfer occurs. After the addition of Cu2+, no new absorption peak appears and only absorptions of PFE-Tc at 397 nm and 265 nm decreased, indicating of a static quenching process from the inner filter effect (IFE) ( Li & Tong, 2020 ). In addition, (Fig. 1 c) after the addition of Cu2+(50 μM/100 μM), the fluorescence lifetimes are 81.49 μs and 79.37 μs respectively. Negligible changes also suggested the fluorescence quenching of PFE-Tc was ascribed to inner filter effect due to the combination of Cu2+ with Tc over Eu3+ and subsequent destruction of antenna effect ( Lu et al., 2017 ).

Meanwhile, photostability of reference fluorescence was evaluate by monitoring the FL intensity at 519 nm with continuous UV exposure. As shown in Fig. S2a, the FL intensities of PFE probe at 519 nm exhibit no significant change within 1 h of exposure. Moreover, the FL intensity at 519 nm increased with from 50 to 80 ℃ (Fig. S2b). Increasing the temperature (20–50 ℃) also has little effect on the FL intensity, implying the thermostability of FL intensities for PFE probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response to Tc and Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to obtain the good sensing performance towards TCs detection, the optimal detection conditions including pH value, the dosage of probes, excitation wavelength and the response time were first explored. The fluorescence intensity ratio (I617/I519) increased with the increase of excitation wavelength, and reached a maximum at 400 nm, which was selected as the optimal excitation (
Fig. 3 a). Then, the pH value was optimized, as shown in Fig. 3 b, the fluorescence intensity ratio (I617/I519) had pH-dependent effect due to protonation-deprotonation ( Li, Fan, et al., 2020b ). The maximum appeared at pH 9, indicating that pH 9 is optimal. The probes dosage played a vital role in the detection sensitivity. Fig. 3 c showed that the ratio of I619/I519 increased with increasing dosage of PFE sensor and the highest detection sensitivity was achieved at 10 ug of PFE (2 µg/mL). In addition, the influence of the response time was also studied (Fig. 3 d), and the ratio of I617/I519 sharply increased within 300 s and reached a plateau, indicating the fast coordination process of Tc with PFE probe.

To evaluate the specificity of fluorescent probes, interfering substances were also tested under the same condition, including common metal ions (K +, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+), representative amino acids and antibiotic (Arg, Cys, Tyr, Lys, His, Gly, Glu, KAN, EM, PG and LFX). As shown in Fig. 5 , negligible signal changes were observed in the present of these interfering in the sensing of PFE and PFE-Tc, revealing good anti-interference ability. As shown in Fig. S4, when heavy metal ions (Mo2+, zn2+, Sn2+) were added, the signal change of PFE-Tc sensing was also negligible, exhibits good resistance to heavy metals. The above results the established probe possess prominent selectivity and strong anti-interference ability towards Tc and Cu2+ detection, suggesting large application potential in on-site quantification in the actual samples.

To evaluate the feasibility and practicality of the PFE sensor, the TCs and Cu2+ were detected in tap water, milk and honey samples by standard addition method. As shown in Table S1 and Table S2, the recoveries of TC were 87.41–95.18 % with RSDs of 0.74–1.49 % in tap water, 96.50–104.0 % in milk with RSDs of 0.62–3.18 %, and 94.30–100.95 % in honey samples with RSDs of 1.70–2.33 %. Similarly, the Cu2+ recoveries from 96.19 % to 102.92 % with RSDs from 1.54 % to 2.15 % in tap water, from 95.96 % to 115.30 %with RSD from 0.69 % to 2.81 % in milk and from 95.19 % to 102.92 % with RSD from 0.91 % to 2.19 % in honey samples were obtained, respectively. The results clearly verify the high accuracy and good repeatability of the PFE-based sensing platform in real sample analysis.

Moreover, the sensing performance of as prepared fluorometric probe for detection of Tc and Cu2+ was compared with previous works as listed in Table 1. This strategy showed has a simpler preparation process and a wider linear range with comparable detection limit, which makes it more practical as a multifunctional fluorescent sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visualization detection of Tc and Cu2+ based on Smartphone
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain a low-cost, rapid, and on-site determination tool, the filter papers loaded with the as-prepared PFE and PFE-Tc probes were further developed as portable sensors for the colorimetric analysis of Tc and Cu2+ under 365 nm. As shown in Fig. 6 a, b, the color of the filter paper changed from cyan and red significantly with Tc increasing. Meanwhile, with the addition of increasing Cu2+, the fluorescence color gradually changed from red to blue. Furthermore, the image was converted by the RGB Color Picker APP into digital values representing red, green, and blue (RGB). The relationship curve between logarithm R/G and the concentration of the Tc and Cu2+ were plotted, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6 c, a good linearity is observed between logarithm R/G and the Tc concentrations (0.5–50 μM). The corresponding regression equation is logarithm R/G = 0.24435[Tc]- 0.1198(R2 = 0.994), and the LOD of Tc is 0.116 μM. Similarly shown in Fig. 6 d, the value of logarithm G/R linearly depends on the Cu2+ concentration in the range of 2 ∼ 100 μM. The linear relationship follows as logarithm G/R = 0.07178 [Cu2+]-0.2692 (R 2 = 0.987), and the limit of detection is 0.480 μM. The results show that the smartphone-assisted ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform has great promising prospects for the real-time colorimetric analysis of Tc and Cu2+. And we also do the paper-based honey sample detection, the results as shown in Table. S3. The recoveries of Tc were 91.58–106.87 % with RSDs of 1.64 –5.09 % and the recoveries of Cu2+ were 102.22–104.83 % with RSDs of 2.18 – 4.91 %, proved the PFE paper-based sensor in the actual sample of rapid and accurate detection of the feasibility.

In summary, PASP-FL-Eu (PFE) were successfully fabricated for detecting Tc and Cu2+ by fluorometric based on the fluorescence “on-off-on” mechanism. The introduction of Tc induced linearly fluorescence increase via antenna effect, and the fluorescence emission can be quenched in the presence of Cu2+ via IFE, thus confirming the stronger affinity between Cu2+ and Tc over Eu3+. The PAsp based ratiometric fluorescent probe exhibits high sensitivity, good selectivity and wide liner range for detection of Tc and Cu2+ and can be used for the rapid detection of TC and Cu2+ in real sample. Moreover, a facile test paper has been developed, and the smartphone with RGB Color Picker was employed for visual and real time testing of Tc and Cu2+. Therefore, the proposed PAsp based ratiometric fluorescent sensor provides a facile, rapid, and visual sensing platform for real-time monitoring of Tc and Cu2+ with high sensitivity and selectivity, which has valuable applications in food safety and environment analysis.
This work was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds of Zhejiang Sci-Tech University（24042127-Y）.
We declare that this manuscript is original, has not been published before and is notcurrently being considered for publication elsewhere.

We confirm that the manuscript has been read and approved by all named authors andthat there are no other persons who satisfied the criteria for authorship but are notlisted. We further confirm that the order of authors listed in the manuscript has beenapproved by all ofus.

We understand that the Corresponding Author is the sole contact for the Editorialprocess. She is responsible for communicating with the other authors about progresssubmissions ofrevisions and final approval of proofs.

Danling Mei: Validation. Lei Wang: Methodology. Biao Huang: Supervision. Xueqin Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Feifan Cheng: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Conceptualization. Yue Huang: Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jfca.2024.106867.
